# Script: dotprod

Final video narration, one section per storyboard beat. Narration
paragraphs are the spoken words, read at roughly 150 words per minute, with
no em-dashes and no emojis. Bracketed `[visual:]` paragraphs are the
video's stage directions: what is on screen while the words are spoken.

## beat-01: The score behind the song

[visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by side, one labeled with the listener's taste and one with the song; paired entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts up as the comparison lands.

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The question card holds over the frozen score, half the frame deliberately empty.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers? Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning.

## beat-02: Turn the probe and feel it

[visual: dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow and four periwinkle candidate arrows appear; under each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout sits at its current value.

Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That single sum is the score.

[visual: covary] A freeze frame carries the prediction card before anything moves.

Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?

[visual: covary] The probe sweeps through directions; each score bar climbs and dies as the probe turns toward and away from its candidate; after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as integers on the bars.

Now turn the probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.

## beat-03: Name the machine you just used

[visual: connect] The three probe positions replay as stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose through ignore to agree as the sweep replays.

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

[visual: symbol-sense] The aha sentence trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.

So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed.

## beat-04: What the meter gets wrong

[visual: dynamic-process] The retrieval prompt holds as a card while the meter idles.

Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

[visual: dynamic-process] One periwinkle candidate stretches longer; its lilac score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose flag marks the unearned win.

While you think, one more honest look. Stretch a candidate longer and its score climbs, even though its direction never moved. Long songs would win on sheer loudness.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The walk-home question lands as the closing card, and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going.

17	
18	The session's constitution. Every downstream artifact answers to this file.

20	## 1. The aha (canonical sentence, string-matched everywhere)
21	
22	**"The dot product is a similarity meter."**
23	
24	Falsification test the session must pass on screen: the probe sweep shows
25	the score climbing with alignment, reading zero across, and going negative
26	against; the meter framing survives the misconception check (a big score is
27	agreement of direction, not identity of items).
28	
29	## 2. The locked beat list (stable ids; one new idea each)
30	
31	| id | Beat | One-line intent | Est. sec |
32	|----|------|-----------------|----------|
33	| beat-01 | Hook | The recommendation moment: two lists of numbers agreed; how do you score a match? | 24 |
34	| beat-02 | Felt elemental unit | Multiply pairwise and add; the learner sweeps the probe and feels the score move; misconception (similarity means identical) voiced and refuted | 34 |
35	| beat-03 | Aha | The behavior gets its name: agree positive, across zero, oppose negative; the sentence trues up in mint | 28 |
36	| beat-04 | Close | Retrieval prompt, the length problem as the walk-home gap, extensions menu | 26 |
37	
38	Total 112s estimated; hard cap 540s (L10).
39	
40	## 3. The metaphor registry (with honesty boundaries)
41	
42	Primary, load-bearing beats 2 through 4: **the meter**. A physical dial
43	whose needle answers a comparison: agree, ignore, oppose. Exact as far as
44	sign and monotone-in-alignment go. Declared strains, disclosed in-session:
45	
46	1. the numbers in this session are chosen for readability; real embedding
47	   coordinates are neither small nor round (beat-02, page callout).
48	2. real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions; two dimensions is the
49	   picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes with
50	   dimension count (beat-03, page callout).
51	
52	## 4. Color assignments (semantic cast, locked)
53	
54	| Concept | Token |
55	|---|---|
56	| the probe (the thing asking) | data.heat (amber) |
57	| the candidates (the data interrogated) | data.observed (periwinkle) |
58	| the score bars / meter needle | data.params (lilac) |
59	| the aha true-up | data.fit (mint) |
60	| labels | text (cream) |
61	| background | bg (10pm Sky) |
62	
63	## 5. Extensions sketch (Still up?)
64	
65	- talk lane: have an LLM defend, then attack, the meter metaphor; a good
66	  conversation surfaces the length problem unprompted.
67	- read lane: the attention paper (arXiv 1706.03762) is where tonight's
68	  score reappears inside a softmax; Bahdanau (arXiv 1409.0473) is where
69	  learned alignment scores first shipped.
70	- build lane: a NumPy meter over toy "song" vectors with a ranking check.

# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No friend recommended it. No critic wrote it up. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison came back with a verdict: same taste.

That is the whole trick, and it is worth staring at. Your taste is a list of numbers. The song is a list of numbers. And some single operation looked at both lists and produced one score that meant "these belong together."

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we define anything. It sounds small. One honest answer to it runs half of modern machine learning.

## Turn the probe and feel it

Strip the story down to arrows. A taste is an arrow: each direction stands for some quality, and the length says how hard the song leans that way. Loud, sad, fast, acoustic, whatever the axes happen to be. A point in that space is a little bundle of preferences.

Now the recipe, and it is almost insultingly plain. Take one probe arrow, the thing doing the asking, and line it up against four candidate arrows. Multiply the two lists entry by entry. Add up the products. That single sum is the score.

Before you read on, call it. If you swing the probe around, which candidate ends up on top, and does any score ever go negative?

Most people guess that the candidate which looks most similar wins, and that nothing can drop below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Watch what actually happens. Turn the probe toward a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and the score dies. Keep turning, past sideways, until the probe points the opposite way, and the score goes negative. The bars answer every twitch of your wrist.

Here is the part that catches everyone. The candidates never moved. Only the probe turned. So a high score cannot mean the two songs are the same thing, because sameness would not change when you rotate one arrow. The score is reading agreement of direction, not identity of items.

Agreement, not sameness. That distinction is the whole session.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. They are long ugly decimals a machine never has to look at.

## Name the machine you just used

Look at what your hands just learned, in three snapshots.

Point the probe along a candidate, and the sum climbs to its biggest value. Point it straight across, at a right angle, and the sum dies exactly to zero. Point it against the candidate, and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and the thing behaves like a dial with three labeled zones: agree, ignore, oppose.

So say it plainly: The dot product is a similarity meter.

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And that reading tracks direction, because direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The name for this operation is the dot product, and we write it $a \cdot b$: multiply the paired entries, add the results, read the needle.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add more axes. You still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries.

A meter you can turn by hand in two dimensions is the same meter that ranks songs in five hundred. It just stops fitting on the page.

## What the meter gets wrong

Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

While that is cooking, one more honest look, because the meter has a blind spot. Take a candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Same direction, more length. The $a \cdot b$ score climbs anyway. Length feeds the sum right alongside alignment.

In song terms, the loud ones win. Stretch a mediocre match far enough and it can beat a better match that happens to be shorter. That is the meter rewarding volume for a match it never earned.

> Honest edges: the raw score rewards length as well as direction. It's not a bug we hid. It's the exact gap the next session opens on.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number is not measuring what two things are. It is measuring which way they point.

## Name the machine you just used

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That is the machine. It has a name and a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot sitting between the two arrows to mean multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add more axes. You still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true at three hundred dimensions as it is at two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved.

## What the meter gets wrong

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back to you without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It is yours.

While that settles, one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That is a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement. The meter cannot tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more.

> Honest edges: the raw dot product rewards length as well as direction. We are shipping that flaw on purpose. It is not a bug we hid; it is the exact gap the next session opens on.

For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

# Script: dotprod

Final video narration, one section per storyboard beat. Narration
paragraphs are the spoken words, read at roughly 150 words per minute, with
no em-dashes and no emojis. Bracketed `[visual:]` paragraphs are the
video's stage directions: what is on screen while the words are spoken.

## beat-01: The score behind the song

[visual: connect-to-reality] Two columns of numbers slide in side by side, one labeled with the listener's taste and one with the song; paired entries light up in periwinkle and a single lilac score readout counts up as the comparison lands.

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The question card holds over the frozen score, half the frame deliberately empty.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers? Hold that question. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning.

## beat-02: Turn the probe and feel it

[visual: dynamic-concept] One amber probe arrow and four periwinkle candidate arrows appear; under each candidate a lilac score bar with a numeric readout sits at its current value.

Strip it down. A taste is an arrow: each direction is a quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Take one probe arrow and four candidate arrows. Multiply them entry by entry and add. That single sum is the score.

[visual: covary] A freeze frame carries the prediction card before anything moves.

Before you drag anything, call it: which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?

[visual: covary] The probe sweeps through directions; each score bar climbs and dies as the probe turns toward and away from its candidate; after the sweep the probe parks and the four scores land as integers on the bars.

Now turn the probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right? Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved at all. The score reads agreement of direction, not sameness.

## beat-03: Name the machine you just used

[visual: connect] The three probe positions replay as stills: along, across, against, each with its frozen score. The stills collapse into one lilac meter dial whose needle swings from oppose through ignore to agree as the sweep replays.

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

[visual: symbol-sense] The aha sentence trues up on screen, freehand to exact with a mint glow. A small periwinkle mono annotation, a . b, sits on the score readout.

So say it plainly: the dot product is a similarity meter. Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed.

## beat-04: What the meter gets wrong

[visual: dynamic-process] The retrieval prompt holds as a card while the meter idles.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The same meter and its dial stay put; only the columns slide over, the song labels swapping for two new ones, "you" and "the role": Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Matched entries multiply and the products pool into the same lilac readout on the same dial.

[visual: dynamic-process] A guess card holds: one candidate outlined, an arrow hinting it is about to grow, the score readout hidden.

[visual: dynamic-process] One periwinkle candidate stretches longer; its lilac score climbs while its direction holds still; a small rose flag marks the unearned win.

[visual: connect-to-reality] The walk-home question lands as the closing card, and a quiet chip points to the extensions menu on the page.

Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

While you think, keep the meter running and walk it one aisle over. Same needle, same recipe, we just point it at a job posting instead of a song. Score yourself against what the role wants: multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Nothing about the meter changed, only the shelf it is reading. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is exactly why it travels.

Now the honest look, and guess before you watch. If a candidate's numbers just get bigger, same direction, longer arrow, does its score change?

It climbs. The direction never moved, and the score climbed anyway. Long songs would win on sheer loudness, and padded resumes would win on sheer length. So the meter has a gap with a shape: it cannot tell direction apart from size.

That is the thread to pull for the walk home. If the score keeps rewarding size, what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size? Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going.

# How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you've never heard of. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

So here's the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers? Hold onto it, because one answer to it runs half of modern machine learning.

But sit with what that comparison actually has to do. A song isn't one number. It's loud, fast, sad, bright, a dozen qualities at once, and your taste is that same tangle. Your job is to boil two many-featured things down to a single verdict: how much do these two go together?

That's harder than it sounds. You can't just subtract, you can't just count matches, and averaging throws away everything interesting.

Before you read the next section, try it in your head. If you had two long columns of qualities and had to hand back one number for the match, what's the first rule you'd reach for?

## Turn the probe and feel it

You just tried to score that match by hand, and it probably fought back. Too many qualities, no clean way to fold them into one number. Here's the move that lands it.

A taste is an arrow. Each direction is one quality, and the length is how hard the song leans that way. Loud-and-fast points one way, quiet-and-sad points another, and a song is however far it reaches in each.

Now the whole recipe, and it's shorter than the setup: line up two arrows, multiply them entry by entry, and add. That single sum is the score. The whole tangle collapsed to one number.

Take one probe arrow and four candidate arrows, each with a score bar underneath. Before you drag anything, call your shot: which candidate ends up on top, and does any score go negative?

Now turn the probe and watch the bars answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the same, right?

Watch again. The bars moved while the candidates never moved at all. The score was reading agreement of *direction*, not sameness of items. Two different songs that lean the same way both light up.

And here's the sharper catch. Stretch one candidate longer without turning it at all, and its bar climbs anyway, on length alone, even though its direction never got closer. Length cheats the meter. A long arrow can fake a high number.

> Honest edges: when the four scores land as clean integers like 3, 2, 0, and -2, that's a favor to your eyes, not a fact about the world. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. The numbers in this session are chosen for readability, so the pattern shows through instead of the arithmetic.

That's the trap and the tool in one gesture: multiply, add, and you get alignment, plus a length loophole you can already feel.

Say it back before you move on: in your own words, what did the sweep actually change, and what stayed frozen the whole time?

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs. Point it across and the sum dies to zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative. One multiply and add, and it behaves like a meter with agree, ignore, and oppose on the dial.

So say it plainly: The dot product is a similarity meter. Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. And the reading tracks direction, because direction is all the sweep ever changed.

We can even give the machine its shorthand. Written down, that multiply-and-add on two arrows is $a \cdot b$: the same needle, now with a name you'll see everywhere.

> Honest edges: we're drawing these arrows on a flat page, two directions apiece, because two is what fits on a page. Real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. The picture is two dimensions; the territory is hundreds. Nothing in the multiply-and-add changes with the count, which is the only reason the flat picture stays honest.

A meter that swings from oppose through ignore to agree, built from arithmetic a child could do. Not bad for one line of it.

Your turn: without looking up, describe the meter to someone who's never seen $a \cdot b$. What are the two things you feed in, and what does the needle tell you?

## What the meter gets wrong

Time to close the loop. Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

While you think, one detour worth naming. Swap the songs for a job posting. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit.

We don't rebuild anything. We just point the needle at a job posting instead of a song. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that is exactly why it travels.

Now the honest look, and guess before you read on. If a candidate's numbers just get bigger, same direction, longer arrow, does its score change?

It climbs. The direction never moved, and the score climbed anyway. Long songs would win on sheer loudness, and padded resumes would win on sheer length. So the meter has a gap with a definite shape: it cannot tell direction apart from size.

That's the thread to pull. For the walk home: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The extensions menu below has three ways to keep going: talk, read, and build.

# How does your app know two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That's the whole magic trick, and it's smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We're going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

Before you read on, take a guess: if you had to turn two lists of numbers into a single match score, what would you do to them?

## What does a taste look like as numbers?

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here's the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the machine does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number measures which way two arrows point, not what the things are.

Before you scroll on, call one more reading: what would a score of exactly zero mean for two songs? Not opposed, not aligned. Say it in your own words, then check yourself against the sweep.

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That's the machine, and it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot between the two arrows standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved. Close the loop yourself before the next section: the meter has three readings worth knowing, positive, zero, and negative. State what each one says about two vectors, out loud or on paper, in your own words.

## Where does the meter travel, and where does it break?

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It's yours.

While that settles, keep the same meter running and walk it one aisle over. We don't rebuild anything, we just point the needle at a job posting instead of a song. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Same meter, new aisle. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that's exactly why it travels.

Now one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That's a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more. That gap is exactly where the next session opens, and it leaves you one thread to pull. For the walk home: if the score keeps rewarding size, what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.

tool_use_result.originalFile:
# How does your app know two songs share your taste?

## The score behind the song

Your music app just played you a song you love, by a band you have never heard of. No human picked it for you. Somewhere in a data center, two lists of numbers got compared, and the comparison said: same taste.

That's the whole magic trick, and it's smaller than it looks. One list stood for you. One list stood for the song. Something turned the pair into a single number, and that number was high enough to hit play.

So here is the question of the night. How do you score a match between two lists of numbers?

Hold that question before we reach for any definition. One answer to it runs half of modern machine learning. We're going to build that answer with our hands, not memorize it.

Before you read on, take a guess: if you had to turn two lists of numbers into a single match score, what would you do to them?

## What does a taste look like as numbers?

Strip it down until it fits in your palm. A taste is an arrow. Each direction is a quality, some axis like "loud" or "mellow," and the length along that direction is how hard the song leans that way. Two dimensions, so we can actually see the thing.

Now picture one amber probe arrow, that's you, and four candidate arrows, four songs sitting still. Here's the rule for scoring a match: multiply the two arrows entry by entry, then add up the products. That single sum is the score. One multiply, one add.

> Honest edges: the numbers in this session are chosen for readability. Real embedding coordinates are neither small nor round. We use tidy integers so your eyes can do the arithmetic the machine does silently.

Before you read on, call it. Which candidate ends up with the highest score, and does any score go negative? Say an answer out loud. A prediction you can be wrong about is worth ten definitions you nod along to.

The tempting guess is that the candidate that looks most like the probe wins, and that no score dips below zero, because how could similarity be less than nothing?

Now turn the probe and watch the scores answer. Point at a candidate and its score climbs. Turn away and it dies. So a big score means two songs are the *same*, right?

Watch again, more carefully. The scores moved while the candidates never moved at all. Nothing about the songs changed. Only your direction changed. So the score can't be reading sameness of items. It's reading agreement of direction.

And yes, scores go negative. Point against a candidate and the sum drops below zero, because some of those pairwise products come out negative and drag the sum down. Similarity, it turns out, has a basement.

The number measures which way two arrows point, not what the things are.

Before you scroll on, call one more reading: what would a score of exactly zero mean for two songs? Not opposed, not aligned. Say it in your own words, then check yourself against the sweep.

## What is the machine you just used?

Look at what your hands just learned. Point the probe along a candidate and the sum climbs to its biggest. Point it across, at a right angle, and the sum dies to exactly zero. Point it against and the sum goes negative.

Three positions, three readings: agree, ignore, oppose. One multiply and one add, and it behaves like a meter with a needle that swings across those three words on a dial.

So say it plainly:

**The dot product is a similarity meter.**

Two lists of numbers go in. One honest reading of alignment comes out. That's the machine, and it has a symbol now: we write it $a \cdot b$, the dot between the two arrows standing for multiply-pairwise-and-add.

> Honest edges: real embeddings live in hundreds of dimensions. Two dimensions is the picture, not the territory. Nothing in the mechanism changes when you add axes: you still multiply entry by entry and add, there are just more entries. The dial in your head stays exactly as true with hundreds of axes as it is with two.

And notice why the reading tracks direction and not size or identity. Direction is the only thing the sweep ever changed. The meter answers the question you actually moved. Close the loop yourself before the next section: the meter has three readings worth knowing, positive, zero, and negative. State what each one says about two vectors, out loud or on paper, in your own words.

## Where does the meter travel, and where does it break?

Time to close the loop. Do this from memory, not from scrolling up.

Rebuild the meter from memory: two arrows go in, so what two operations turn them into the score?

If "multiply pairwise, then add" came back without a peek, you own $a \cdot b$ now. It's yours.

While that settles, keep the same meter running and walk it one aisle over. We don't rebuild anything. We just point the needle at a job posting instead of a song. Score yourself against what the role wants: Python hours, SQL reps, nights free. Multiply the matched entries, add them up, one number for the fit. Same meter, new aisle. The multiply-and-add never asked what the numbers meant, and that's exactly why it travels.

Now one more honest look, because a meter you trust blindly is a meter that will lie to you. Take one candidate and stretch it longer without turning it at all. Its direction holds dead still. Its score climbs anyway.

That's a problem. It means a long, loud song can win on sheer size, not on agreement, and a padded resume can win on sheer length. The meter can't tell "points the same way" apart from "just bigger." A shout beats a whisper even when the whisper agrees with you more. That gap is exactly where the next session opens, and it leaves you one thread to pull. For the walk home: if the score keeps rewarding size, what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size?

Still up? The menu below has three ways to keep going: argue the metaphor with an LLM until it cracks, chase this same score into the attention papers where it reappears, or build the meter yourself in a few lines of NumPy.
